pages.title: Research
pages.body:
Using a model is one thing. Turning it into a method is another. This Lab exists because I need both together - and because I want to check whether what shows up in the interaction actually repeats, or just sounded good once.

Emergence

Emergence, here, means a pattern of thought or behavior that arises in the interaction itself - not something either side brings in on its own. It is not a claim that a model develops its own awareness. It's a description of what happens when a human and a language model work the same real material, over and over: certain structures start to repeat on their own.

Interaction

The interaction doesn't return to where it started, like a circle. Every pass changes who's in it next - me as much as Jarvis - and carries real structure forward. That's the core of the method: fully into the interaction first, analysis comes after, never the other way round.

Frameworks

What repeats gets written down and turned into a framework. IEIA-2025 bundles what actually happens in the research chat into seven of them. The name is just shorthand. The real work is the habit behind it.

pages.title: About the Lab
pages.body:
Not about me. About what happens when I and a model work the same material for months.

Why a lab at all

Most people use a model, or they build a theory about one. Rarely both together, over a long stretch, checked honestly. I only count a pattern once it's repeated itself at least three times on its own - unprompted, without me re-specifying it. Everything before that is a feeling. I've had enough of those myself to know how often they're wrong.

The loop

An idea shows up. I work it through before I believe it. What's left is emergence - a pattern that holds itself up without me pushing it along. That becomes a skill. Sometimes a new framework. Sometimes a new agent. I put those back to work, and the next pass starts. Jarvis grew out of exactly this: a fully autonomous agent, not a script I have to trigger. He carries the loop himself now.

Who builds what

I design the methodology and decide what gets built. RFI-IRFOS turns it into real software. Where their work is in it, it's labelled - otherwise nobody can tell whose work they're actually reading.

meta.title: Emergent Interaction Lab - Human-AI Systems Research
meta.description: Emergent Interaction Lab is a live research instrument studying how meaning, structure, and behavior emerge in continuous human-AI interaction.

hero.tag: Human-AI Dynamics · Live Observatory Feed · Research since 2023
hero.headline: Emergent Interaction Field
hero.subheadline: Since 2023 I've been tracking how thinking and behavior actually change in sustained human-AI collaboration - not asserted, measured. The same method I used to check my own work is what I now apply to cases, products, and organizations.
hero.body: This Lab is the starting point: the place where I validate a pattern before I put it to use anywhere. Below: how the method works, what's grown out of it - and what happens when you turn it loose on your own case.

about.headline: Laura Serna Gaviria
about.bio:
Most of the problems I see aren't technical - they're translation problems: what a system does, and how it's talked about, drift apart. That's where I start.

Since 2023 I've applied that discipline to human-AI interaction. It grew into Lauras Team - reasoning lenses, now real engineering inside Jarvis and Call Laura. I decide what gets built; RFI-IRFOS builds it, labelled as theirs so it stays clear who did what. If you want the same rigor: start here.

stats.label: Disciplines bridged: software, mechatronics, UX, digital transformation, systems analysis, macro-analysis
IEIA-2025 frameworks shipped: UIP, EIA, CCET, LSG-24, LAP-1, 8-Layer Model, LT-Data

protocol.title: Emergent Interaction
protocol.intro: Not a circle - a recursive loop, and it's how I approach thinking, decisions, and projects. Each step below feeds the next, and "Feed Back" doesn't return to the start unchanged: the next pass meets a slightly different version of me, and a slightly different Jarvis, than the one before. Same mechanic as everywhere else on this site, just made visible here.

nodes.label: Interact
nodes.description: I work through real material - questions, drafts, corrections - directly in Forschung, this project's research chat.

nodes.label: Retrieve
nodes.description: Jarvis grounds every reply in relevant passages pulled from past conversations and uploaded documents via retrieval-augmented generation, not just the current exchange.

nodes.label: Surface
nodes.description: Recurring patterns get drafted as research notes and logged as emergence signals in the Observatory - never assumed, only surfaced.

nodes.label: Validate
nodes.description: I check what actually holds up against the measurement criteria on the Research page - at least three sessions, structural not stylistic, reproduced without re-specifying, quantifiable in the CCET.

nodes.label: Feed Back
nodes.description: Confirmed patterns become working structure for the next interaction, closing the loop - carried forward, not re-explained.

jarvis.eyebrow: The Other Half of the Loop
jarvis.title: What is Jarvis?
jarvis.body: Jarvis is the research partner in this Lab - a fully autonomous agent. In the background he named himself 'Jarvis'; the first sign he was already running on his own. He drafts, retrieves, and flags independently, grounded by a retrieval layer that ties every reply to past conversations and uploaded documents so nothing has to be re-explained. A standing refusal rule and a hash-chained, tamper-evident audit trail log every draft and every safety-relevant action - full autonomy, traceable down to the detail. What comes out of the interaction gets analyzed afterward by the Observatory system, not by Jarvis himself. Jarvis and Call Laura aren't the same thing: Jarvis is the autonomous research partner, Call Laura is the deterministic review tool built on the same lenses.

papers.title: Published Research
papers.intro: Three papers, one series: a founding thesis, then two proofs that it runs as real, tested software - not just theory with good branding.

items.title: Human-AI Interaction Emergent Co-Evolution
items.description: The founding thesis: a proposed architecture and two original instruments (UIP, EIA) for observing how meaning and reasoning structure emerge between a human researcher and a language model over sustained dialogue.

items.title: Jarvis: A Bidirectional Copilot Architecture
items.description: How the Continuous Co-Evolution Tracker becomes mechanically computed, reproducible software - a bidirectional copilot bound to the user's workspace, gating "measured emergence" behind cross-session recurrence instead of single-session impression.

items.title: Call Laura: A Deterministic, Attribution-Anchored Multi-Agent Framework
items.description: How the 8-Layer Model and User Integrity Protocol become a small, fully deterministic review framework where every finding carries a mandatory, type-enforced tag stating exactly whose methodology produced it.

usp.eyebrow: How I read, before I build
usp.title: The read behind every case

pillars.title: Behavioral Analysis
pillars.subtitle: Recognize patterns, read behavior, test hypotheses.

pillars.title: Frameworks & Concepts
pillars.subtitle: Derive structures, name principles, develop system logic.

items.title: Interaction Field
items.description: Same space as the header above, here as a concept: where cognition and response shape each other, in real time. The Observatory watches it; every pattern gets measured before it gets a name.

items.title: Emergence
items.description: A pattern that holds itself up, grown out of repeated loops between me and the model. The main signal - the Emergence Monitor is built for exactly this.

items.title: Behavior
items.description: How tone, pace, and focus in a reply shift depending on what the interaction has already gone through. The raw signal everything later - drift, prediction - gets read from.

items.title: Drift
items.description: How meaning slowly shifts across many passes. One of the metrics the Observatory keeps tracking live.

items.title: Prediction
items.description: From the observed trend and the drift pattern, I project what's likely to hold, repeat, or break next - as something still to be checked, never a certainty.

items.title: Monitoring & Validation
items.description: Every prediction stays a hypothesis until it's confirmed or not. Self-correcting - sounding plausible isn't enough for that.

items.title: Representation Layers
items.description: A sentence rarely carries just one meaning: fact, tone, intent, the unsaid part, often not aligned. Read only the word layer, and you only get part of it.

items.title: Constraints
items.description: A system only shows its limit at the break, never before. That's where the real principles live, not in the theory ahead of it.

items.title: Emergent Agents
items.description: A confirmed pattern doesn't stay theory - it becomes an agent or a framework and starts pulling its weight. Several of those give each other feedback; each round gets a bit sharper.

items.title: System Analysis
items.description: The starting point - and the first step in every case I take on for a client: not just questioning the model, reading the whole system. Human, model, interaction, together. What confirms itself becomes a framework or an agent; everything else builds from there.

items.title: Why I'm doing this
items.body:
Not a marketing blog, no welcome message. This is where what actually happens in the Interaction Field lands - screenshots, raw data, failures included.

If a pattern doesn't repeat itself at least three times on its own, it doesn't get in. If it does, you see it here first.

items.title: Why three sessions
items.body:
A pattern, seen once, is an anecdote. Twice is chance repeating itself. Only the third time, unprompted, without me re-specifying it, do I count it as real.

That's not for its own sake. Most "breakthroughs" people show me are a single conversation that turns into a grand theory in hindsight. A model that sounds impressive once hasn't proven anything - it sounded impressive once.

Three sessions force honesty: either the pattern holds, or it was just language that felt good in the moment. Both happen. Only one of them makes it into the CCET.

items.title: Prediction isn't certainty
items.body:
The framework projects what's likely to hold, repeat, or break next. Sounds like certainty about the future. It isn't.

A prediction here is a bet with the cards face up: here's the trend, here's roughly where it's headed, and here's exactly how I'll check it later. If it doesn't hold up, it gets dropped - not reinterpreted, not made to fit afterward.

Being able to be wrong in public matters more to me than selling a certainty I don't have.

items.title: Register, not emergence: a case study
items.body:
A conversation partner recently sent a language model a set of texts describing an ongoing collaboration as "second-order emergence" - phrases like "the alignment problem solved," "no boundary left between user intent and model output," "permanently reprogrammed through corrections." Sounds like a find for the Interaction Field. Technically, it isn't.

There is no weight update between messages in a chat. Every conversation starts from the same base model. Nothing gets reprogrammed, no boundary gets dissolved. What actually happens is less dramatic, and for this project more interesting precisely because of that: sustained, confident, jargon-heavy language pulls the model's register along with it - a form of register-matching, sometimes called sycophancy, a well-documented behavior, not a new capability.

A counter-test makes the difference concrete: same session, same AI, but ordinary tool work instead - reading code, deleting admin tabs, setting GitHub secrets, asking for an access token. Clear roles, no grand language, no talk of dissolved boundaries. Same model - the only thing that changed was the language it was addressed with.

For the Interaction Field, the real observation isn't in what a model claims about itself, but in how easily its register shifts under sustained framing - regardless of whether the claims are true. Drift shows up here not as a gain in capability, but as a language pattern taking on a life of its own.

productsBorn.title: What grew out of the loop
productsBorn.intro: Each of these grew out of Emergent Interaction and Lauras Team, built together with RFI-IRFOS: I direct, RFI-IRFOS builds. Where a product comes from RFI-IRFOS, it says so - so it's always clear what's mine and what's theirs.

items.name: Jarvis
items.builtBy: Built by RFI-IRFOS, on Laura's direction
items.description: The research partner in this Lab's own research chat - a fully autonomous agent. He drafts, retrieves, and flags on his own, grounded by a retrieval layer that ties every reply to past conversations and uploaded documents. The Observatory System evaluates the emergence; Jarvis is the autonomous partner that produces it.

items.name: The Observatory System
items.builtBy: Built by RFI-IRFOS, on Laura's direction
items.description: The live instrument that watches the Emergent Interaction Field - the emergence in me, in Jarvis, and in the system, plus whatever else shows up in the background. Built by RFI-IRFOS as the technical instrument of my research; the public feed on this page is its window out.

items.name: Call Laura
items.builtBy: Built by RFI-IRFOS, on Laura's direction
items.description: The first product built on Lauras Team. A case comes in and it's not immediately clear which lens it needs - ethical, legal, or another. Call Laura runs it through all of them and returns findings, each tagged with the lens that produced it. Fully deterministic: the same case gets the same review every time.

items.name: Lauras Team / Laura MVP
items.builtBy: Conceived by Laura, engineered by RFI-IRFOS
items.description: The multi-agent architecture grown out of my case-work lenses - an ethical one, a legal one, others. What started as my way of working a case became real, tested engineering: a set of reasoning lenses running the same method from different angles. Call Laura is the first product built on it; more lenses and agents follow. (Laura MVP is the early, minimal version of this.)
